refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall support redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths with automatic failover and link health monitoring
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop redundancy and dynamic route optimization
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary link with secondary satellite/cellular backup and continuous availability monitoring
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid approach—primary terrestrial link (e.g., licensed RF or cellular) with secondary satellite/cellular backup, continuous link health monitoring, automatic failover logic, and real-time availability telemetry.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure (external dependency on carrier/ISP uptime).
Failover logic couples to Safety Services (safe state must be triggered on link loss).
Monitoring and telemetry couple to Vehicle state machine (must support graceful degradation).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via clear interface contracts (SafetyAlert, Telemetry). DP-D is decoupled from specific vehicle payload or mission logic, making it reusable across NGHE variants.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Secondary link (satellite/cellular) is available at the worksite and has sufficient bandwidth for telemetry.
Risk: Failover latency may exceed safe state transition time; mitigation required via FR-NGHE-010.2.
Risk: Dual-link cost and power consumption may impact vehicle design; trade-off analysis needed.
Assumption: Link health monitoring can reliably distinguish transient glitches from true link loss.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to specify: (1) primary link technology and SLA, (2) secondary link technology and activation threshold, (3) failover latency budget (must be < safe state transition time), (4) monitoring interval and link-loss detection threshold (e.g., 3 consecutive missed heartbeats).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the teleoperation link availability solution: dual-path communication stack, link health monitoring, failover controller, and telemetry aggregation. Interfaces with Vehicle for safe state commands and with Safety Services for link-loss alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits link state, signal strength, failover events, and availability metrics to monitoring/logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (via tele-operator) and forwards them to Vehicle control systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits link-loss alerts to Safety Services to trigger safe state transition.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module manages primary and secondary network links (terrestrial and satellite/cellular) for bidirectional command-telemetry exchange.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own link availability logic. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across multiple sub-systems and obscure ownership of failover and monitoring logic.

Vehicle module owns vehicle control and actuation. Link availability is a communication concern, not a vehicle dynamics concern. Assigning to Vehicle would couple communication infrastructure to vehicle design and create diffusion of responsibility.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of link availability. It owns the communication stack, link monitoring, and failover logic. High cohesion: all link-related concerns are contained. Low diffusion: no scattering of link availability logic across other modules.

Safety Services owns safe state policies and alerts but does not own the communication infrastructure. Assigning link availability to Safety Services would conflate communication reliability with safety policy, creating tight coupling and diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the sole owner of teleoperation link availability. It encapsulates dual-path communication, link health monitoring, failover logic, and telemetry emission. It interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking for commands, SafetyAlert for link-loss triggers) and with external monitoring systems (via Telemetry). This assignment achieves high cohesion and low diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it must handle all link-related logic for multiple vehicle types. Mitigation: design Teleoperation as a reusable, configurable module with pluggable link technologies.

Risk: Failover logic in Teleoperation must coordinate with Vehicle safe state machine. If Vehicle safe state transition is slow, failover may not prevent unsafe conditions. Mitigation: define clear latency budgets and interface contracts (SafetyAlert).

Risk: Secondary link (satellite/cellular) may have different latency/bandwidth characteristics than primary link. Teleoperation must adapt gracefully. Mitigation: design link abstraction layer to hide technology differences.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module includes: (1) primary link driver (e.g., RF or cellular), (2) secondary link driver (e.g., satellite), (3) link health monitor, (4) failover controller, (5) telemetry aggregator. Define clear interface contracts for Tasking (command format, latency), SafetyAlert (link-loss trigger threshold), and Telemetry (availability metrics).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid dual-path with failover and monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 availability). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach (primary + secondary link, failover logic, monitoring) that is traceable to the FR. No logical gaps or contradictions.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic in Teleoperation must coordinate with Vehicle safe state machine; interface contract (SafetyAlert) must be clear and testable.

Link health monitoring in Teleoperation depends on external network infrastructure (carrier SLAs); Teleoperation cannot guarantee availability if external links are unavailable. Mitigation: define SLA assumptions and acceptance criteria.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). All link-related logic is localized. External interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link) are well-defined and traceable to DP-D.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via continuous link state monitoring and post-evaluation statistical analysis. Teleoperation module can emit Telemetry (link state, failover events) for audit. Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns link availability. Vehicle owns safe state transition (triggered by SafetyAlert from Teleoperation). Safety Services owns safe state policies. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine FR-NGHE-010 to specify link-loss detection threshold (e.g., 3 consecutive missed heartbeats) and failover latency budget (must be < safe state transition time).
Refine DP-D to specify primary and secondary link technologies, SLA assumptions, and failover activation criteria.
Define interface contracts for SafetyAlert (link-loss trigger format, latency) and Telemetry (availability metrics, logging format).
Conduct trade-off analysis: dual-link cost/power vs. availability gain. Confirm customer acceptance of DP-D approach.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, safe state transition, redundant paths, audit logging). DP-D is sound and well-scoped, and Teleoperation is the correct IM owner. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to define concrete design parameters (e.g., detection threshold, failover latency, link technologies). Proceeding to the next level will refine the design and reduce risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds (to be refined with latency budget from Vehicle safe state transition time).
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss (owned by Vehicle, triggered by SafetyAlert from Teleoperation).
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall support redundant communication paths (primary terrestrial + secondary satellite/cellular) to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions and failover events for availability audit.

assumptions: Secondary link (satellite/cellular) is available at the worksite with sufficient bandwidth for telemetry.
Link health monitoring can reliably distinguish transient glitches from true link loss via heartbeat mechanism.
Vehicle safe state transition time is known and can be used to set link-loss detection threshold.
Customer accepts dual-link cost and power consumption trade-off.
External network infrastructure (carrier SLAs) meets availability assumptions.
open_risks: Failover latency may exceed safe state transition time; requires latency budget refinement and testing.
Secondary link may have different latency/bandwidth characteristics; Teleoperation must adapt gracefully.
Dual-link cost and power consumption may impact vehicle design; trade-off analysis needed.
Link health monitoring algorithm must be robust to transient glitches; false positives could trigger unnecessary failovers.
evidence_needed: Vehicle safe state transition time (latency budget for link-loss detection).
Primary and secondary link technology specifications (latency, bandwidth, availability SLAs).
Failover activation criteria and latency measurements (lab and field testing).
Link health monitoring algorithm validation (robustness to glitches, false positive rate).
Cost/power trade-off analysis for dual-link approach.
Customer acceptance of DP-D approach and availability target.
